threats.title: Marketplace Freeze Prevents Emergency Unlisting Including by Admin
threats.hypothesis_description: The unlistVesting function has the isFreeze modifier, which prevents ALL callers (including s2Admin) from unlisting when the marketplace is frozen. This contradicts the emergency design intent where the admin should be able to unlist vestings during a freeze to protect user funds. Sellers' listed vesting tokens are locked indefinitely during freeze.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isFreeze modifier fires before the function body executes. The admin authorization check is inside the function body. During freeze, the modifier reverts before reaching the admin check, making admin unlisting impossible without first unfreezing (which creates an insecure window).

proof_of_signal.pattern: function unlistVesting(...) external isFreeze { — modifier blocks all callers before admin check at line 217

preconditions: Admin has frozen the marketplace via setMarketplaceStatus(true)
Active listings exist with non-zero balances

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: isFreeze modifier on unlistVesting prevents admin emergency recovery
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Documentation states admin should be able to delist vestings during emergency, but code prevents it
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Admin bypass of freeze check for unlistVesting is missing

analysis_questions: Can admin workaround by unfreezing, unlisting, refreezing atomically?
During the unfreeze window, can other users exploit the temporary unfreeze?
Are there scenarios where the admin cannot unfreeze (key loss, governance delay)?
validation_requirements: Attempt admin unlistVesting during freeze and confirm revert
Verify that all listed tokens are inaccessible during freeze
Test unfreeze-unlist-refreeze sequence for race conditions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin freezes marketplace due to emergency. All sellers with active listings cannot recover their vesting allocations. Admin must unfreeze to perform emergency unlisting, but this window allows other marketplace operations (purchases, new listings) that the freeze was meant to prevent.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All listed vesting tokens locked during freeze with no emergency recovery path
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All sellers with active listings at time of freeze
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires unfreezing marketplace, creating security window

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for admin unlisting during marketplace freeze

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Obvious when admin attempts to unlist during freeze

threats.title: Missing discountPct Upper Bound Creates Unpurchasable Listings Trapping Seller Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: listVesting does not validate that discountPct < BASE (10000). If a seller sets discountPct >= BASE with FIX or LINEAR discount type, _getDiscountedPrice will underflow on (BASE - discountPct), reverting every purchase attempt. The seller's vesting tokens are trapped in the marketplace until they unlist (potentially paying a penalty fee).
threats.investigation_rationale: The only discount validation is `(_discountType != DiscountType.NO && _discountPct > 0) || (_discountType == DiscountType.NO)`, which accepts any positive value. In _getDiscountedPrice, FIX uses `BASE - listing.discountPct` and LINEAR uses `BASE - (amount * discountPct / total)`. Both underflow if the subtracted value >= BASE.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(discountPct < BASE) at listing creation; _getDiscountedPrice line 266: BASE - listing.discountPct underflows for discountPct >= 10000

preconditions: Seller creates listing with discountType != NO and discountPct >= 10000
Listing is created successfully (no validation blocks it)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing upper bound check on discountPct parameter
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: discountPct=10001 with FIX discount causes BASE-10001 underflow in Solidity 0.8, permanent revert on purchase
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(_discountPct < BASE) or similar bound check

analysis_questions: Can discountPct values just below BASE create near-zero prices enabling extremely cheap purchases?
Is there a griefing vector where attackers create many unpurchasable listings?
Does LINEAR discount have additional edge cases with specific amount/total ratios?
validation_requirements: Create listing with discountPct=10001 and FIX type, verify purchase reverts
Create listing with discountPct=9999 and verify near-zero pricing
Test LINEAR with high discountPct and various amount values

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Seller accidentally or maliciously sets discountPct=15000 with FIX discount. Listing is created successfully. All purchase attempts revert due to underflow. Seller must pay penalty fee to unlist early, or wait for minListingDuration to pass.
prerequisites: Any user who can list vestings

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Seller's tokens trapped; penalty fee required for recovery; potential griefing of marketplace
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Listing seller; potential marketplace usability degradation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Seller can unlist but may incur penalty fee

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary testing for discountPct values

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction to create bad listing

threats.title: PARTIAL Listing minPurchaseAmt Not Validated Against Listing Amount
threats.hypothesis_description: For PARTIAL listings, minPurchaseAmt is not validated to be <= _amount during listVesting. A seller can set minPurchaseAmt > _amount, creating a listing where the only valid purchase is _amount == listing.balance (the full remaining amount), effectively making it a SINGLE-type listing while bypassing SINGLE's explicit amount validation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement `_listingType != ListingType.SINGLE || (_minPurchaseAmt > 0 && _minPurchaseAmt <= _amount)` only validates SINGLE listings. For PARTIAL, any minPurchaseAmt is accepted. In _validatePurchase, the check is `_amount >= listing.minPurchaseAmt || _amount == listing.balance`, so only buying the exact remaining balance works when minPurchaseAmt > balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation of minPurchaseAmt only for SINGLE type; PARTIAL type passes the require regardless of minPurchaseAmt value

preconditions: Seller creates PARTIAL listing with minPurchaseAmt > listing amount

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing bound check: no require(minPurchaseAmt <= _amount) for PARTIAL listings
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: PARTIAL listing with minPurchaseAmt=200 and amount=100 — only purchase of exactly 100 (full balance) is valid
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No PARTIAL-specific validation of minPurchaseAmt relative to listing amount

analysis_questions: Can intermediate purchases leave dust below minPurchaseAmt, griefing the seller?
Does this create permanently stuck listings?
What is the user experience impact?
validation_requirements: Create PARTIAL listing with minPurchaseAmt > amount, verify purchase constraints
Create PARTIAL listing, buy most tokens, verify remaining dust below minPurchaseAmt
Verify dust can only be bought as exact balance match

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Buyer purchases most of a PARTIAL listing, leaving a tiny remainder below minPurchaseAmt. Only someone buying the exact remaining balance can purchase. This griefs the seller who must unlist (with potential penalty) to recover dust.
prerequisites: Any seller creating a PARTIAL listing

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Seller tokens potentially stuck or requiring penalty fee to recover
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Seller can unlist but may incur penalty

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case test for minPurchaseAmt vs listing amount

threats.title: Penalty Fee Denomination Mismatch With 6-Decimal USDT Makes Early Delisting Impossible
threats.hypothesis_description: penaltyFee is initialized to `10 ether` (10 * 10^18) in MarketplaceSetting's constructor. If the configured USDT token has 6 decimals (standard on Ethereum, Base, Arbitrum), this represents 10 trillion USDT. The deployment script (DeploySecondSwap.s.sol) does not call setPenaltyFee after deployment. Early delisting becomes impossible for all sellers until admin manually fixes the fee.
threats.investigation_rationale: The constructor sets `penaltyFee = 10 ether`. USDT on target chains (Ethereum, Base, Arbitrum) has 6 decimals. 10 * 10^18 / 10^6 = 10^13 USDT = 10 trillion USDT. No user can afford this penalty fee.
deployment_assumptions: Admin does not call setPenaltyFee after deployment
threats.config_evidence: SecondSwap_MarketplaceSetting.sol constructor line: penaltyFee = 10 ether; DeploySecondSwap.s.sol does not call setPenaltyFee post-deployment; Target chains use 6-decimal USDT

proof_of_signal.pattern: penaltyFee = 10 ether — uses 18-decimal denomination for a 6-decimal token

preconditions: USDT token has 6 decimals
Admin has not called setPenaltyFee to adjust the value post-deployment

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hardcoded penalty fee uses ether denomination (10^18) mismatched with USDT decimals (10^6)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: 10 * 10^18 USDT with 6 decimals = 10 trillion USDT penalty
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No deployment script step to calibrate penalty fee for token decimals

analysis_questions: Does the deployment script ever call setPenaltyFee?
What is the default USDT token on target chains?
How long could this DoS persist before admin notice?
validation_requirements: Check deployment script for setPenaltyFee call
Verify USDT decimals on target chains
Test unlistVesting with the default penalty fee

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Protocol deploys to Base with 6-decimal USDT. Default penaltyFee = 10e18 USDT. Any seller who lists and wants to unlist before minListingDuration passes must pay 10 trillion USDT, which is impossible. Sellers' tokens are locked until minListingDuration passes or admin fixes the fee.
prerequisites: Default configuration deployed without admin intervention on penalty fee

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All early unlisting blocked; seller tokens locked for minListingDuration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All marketplace sellers during the misconfigured period
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can fix via setPenaltyFee; no permanent fund loss

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test validating penalty fee against actual USDT decimals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately detectable when first seller tries to unlist early

threats.title: setMaxSellPercent Has No Upper Bound Allowing Token Issuer to Bypass Sell Restrictions
threats.hypothesis_description: VestingManager.setMaxSellPercent accepts any uint256 value without upper bound validation. The tokenIssuer can set maxSellPercent to values far exceeding BASE (10000), effectively allowing all original allocation holders to sell 100% of their vesting, bypassing the intended supply restriction mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function has no require statement limiting maxSellPercent to <= BASE. The sell limit formula `((currentAlloc + sold - bought) * maxSellPercent) / BASE` with maxSellPercent > BASE would yield a limit exceeding the original allocation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setMaxSellPercent(address vesting, uint256 maxSellPercent) external — no require(maxSellPercent <= BASE)

preconditions: Caller is the tokenIssuer of the specific vesting plan
tokenIssuer sets maxSellPercent > 10000

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No upper bound check on maxSellPercent
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: maxSellPercent = type(uint256).max allows 100%+ of allocation to be sold

analysis_questions: Can selling more than 100% cause underflows in allocation tracking?
Does this affect the VestingManager's available balance calculations?
Can this be exploited by the tokenIssuer to manipulate token markets?
validation_requirements: Set maxSellPercent to 20000, verify user can sell > 100% of original allocation
Check allocation accounting after overselling
Verify available() balance constraints still prevent actual over-transfer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Token issuer sets maxSellPercent to 50000 (500%). Users can sell up to 500% of their original allocation if they have the tokens. While available() limits actual transfers, the sell limit tracking becomes meaningless, undermining the protocol's supply control mechanism.
prerequisites: Token issuer intentionally or accidentally sets high maxSellPercent

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Secondary market supply controls bypassed; potential token price impact
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders in the affected vesting plan
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Token issuer can reset to correct value

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary test for maxSellPercent values

threats.title: WhitelistDeployer is permissionless: any attacker can deploy arbitrary whitelist contracts (spoofing/private-sale confusion)
threats.hypothesis_description: SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist() is external and permissionless. An attacker can deploy look-alike whitelist contracts with misleading parameters/events (e.g., same lotOwner) and potentially trick integrators/off-chain services that rely on emitted WhitelistCreated events from the deployer address rather than the actual listing.whitelist stored in Marketplace.
threats.investigation_rationale: While Marketplace uses the configured whitelistDeployer address and stores the resulting whitelist address per listing, many production systems index factory events to infer which whitelists are 'official'. Permissionless factory calls can create event noise and spoofed 'official' whitelists, leading to practical integration and access-control failures.
deployment_assumptions: Off-chain indexers/UI/backend treat any WhitelistCreated event from the canonical SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer as authoritative for private lots (instead of reading Marketplace.listings[...].whitelist)
Users rely on those off-chain lists to decide eligibility or to join the whitelist
threats.config_evidence: No on-chain registry check exists in code; risk depends on off-chain consumption of SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer events.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external deployWhitelist() has no access control; emits WhitelistCreated for any caller

preconditions: At least one downstream component (UI/indexer/backoffice) trusts factory events without cross-checking listing.whitelist
Attacker can call deployWhitelist() (permissionless)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Event-based spoofing can mislead private-sale eligibility tooling
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No caller restriction; no linkage to a specific marketplace/listing ID in event

analysis_questions: Do official frontends/indexers derive whitelist addresses from events rather than listing storage?
Is the WhitelistCreated event in Marketplace used as the only authoritative signal instead (safer)?
Are there any on-chain checks that the whitelist is tied to a listing?
validation_requirements: Review subgraph/indexer logic (if any) for whitelist discovery
Ensure UI always reads Marketplace.listings[vestingPlan][listingId].whitelist
Attempt spoof deployment and verify whether any system mistakenly recognizes it

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys multiple whitelists via the canonical deployer, setting lotOwner to a known seller/admin to appear legitimate, then shares the whitelist address to victims/integrators; victims may whitelist themselves on the wrong contract and fail eligibility checks later, or private-sale gating may be misapplied by an indexer.
prerequisites: No additional signature/attestation scheme tying whitelist to listing

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: failed purchases, user funds wasted on gas, private-sale access confusion
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Private sale participants and sellers relying on whitelist tooling
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational; fix indexers/UI; not easily remediated on-chain

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing integration tests validating whitelist discovery source of truth

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare event-derived whitelist lists vs Marketplace storage

threats.title: Whitelist capacity cannot be reduced and is only increasable, enabling permanent griefing of private sales via pre-filling
threats.hypothesis_description: In SecondSwap_Whitelist, anyone can self-whitelist until totalWhitelist == maxWhitelist. The lotOwner can only increase maxWhitelist (cannot decrease or clear entries). An attacker (or group) can pre-fill a private whitelist to max, preventing intended participants from joining; the seller cannot remove malicious entries and must increase maxWhitelist, effectively diluting access control.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even ignoring the obvious lack of access control, the inability to remove or lower limits makes the whitelist irrecoverable once spammed. This is a practical, repeatable DoS against private lots and affects protocol integrity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setMaxWhitelist requires _maxWhitelist > maxWhitelist and > totalWhitelist; no removal mechanism exists

preconditions: A private listing exists with a deployed whitelist contract
Attacker(s) can call whitelistAddress() repeatedly from multiple EOAs/contracts until capacity reached
lotOwner does not have a way to prune/remove spam entries

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Monotonic-only whitelist capacity with no prune/removal
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Once filled, private sale participation is blocked unless lotOwner increases capacity
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No remove function; no allowlist-admin add-only function; no signature-based gating

analysis_questions: Is whitelistAddress() intended to be self-service, or should lotOwner curate?
Do sellers rely on maxWhitelist as a strict cap for KYC/OTC participants?
Are there any off-chain controls preventing sybil pre-fill?
validation_requirements: Simulate sybil fill to maxWhitelist and confirm intended buyers cannot join
Confirm lotOwner cannot reset or invalidate entries
Assess whether Marketplace checks anything besides validateAddress()

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Before or during a private sale, attacker sybil-fills the whitelist to maxWhitelist. Legitimate buyers cannot whitelist and therefore cannot purchase. Seller must either cancel listing (penalty) or increase capacity, effectively losing private-sale selectivity.
prerequisites: Ability to create many addresses (cheap on L2s)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Seller loses ability to execute private sale; potential opportunity loss; gas griefing for buyers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Sellers running private lots and intended whitelisted buyers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Increase maxWhitelist (dilution) or abandon listing; no on-chain cleanup

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for whitelist spam resistance and whitelist lifecycle management

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor totalWhitelist approaching maxWhitelist; identify sybil patterns

threats.title: Initialize functions do not validate critical addresses, enabling permanent bricking if initialized with wrong marketplaceSetting/manager/token
threats.hypothesis_description: SecondSwap_Marketplace.initialize() and SecondSwap_VestingManager.initialize() accept addresses without non-zero or contract checks. If initialized incorrectly (zero addresses or wrong contracts), core flows revert or become irreparably miswired (e.g., marketplaceSetting points to EOA causing isFreeze to revert; vestingManager has wrong s2Admin).
threats.investigation_rationale: Although proxies are initialized via deployment scripts, upgrades (upgradeAndCall) or operational mistakes can re-run initialization on new implementation via delegatecall with bad parameters. This is a common real-world failure mode leading to protocol downtime.
deployment_assumptions: Admin may upgrade and call initialize-like logic on new implementation with incorrect parameters (as in UpgradeMarkeplaceProxy.ts pattern)
No additional access-controlled reinitializer exists; initialize is used as upgrade initialization
threats.config_evidence: UpgradeMarkeplaceProxy.ts encodes initialize() during upgradeAndCall; Marketplace.initialize has no sanity checks on _marketplaceSetting and token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initialize sets isTokenSupport[_token]=true and marketplaceSetting=_marketplaceSetting without require(_marketplaceSetting!=0) or code-size checks

preconditions: Proxy initialization/upgrade uses these initialize functions (delegatecall)
Initializer is invoked with incorrect or malicious parameters (operational error or compromised admin key)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing input validation for critical dependency addresses
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Misconfiguration can brick isFreeze checks and marketplace/manager wiring

analysis_questions: Are these initialize functions ever used as upgrade initializers (re-invoked on upgrade)?
Are there safeguards in ProxyAdmin processes to prevent incorrect parameters?
Would miswiring be recoverable via setMarketplaceSettingAddress/setMarketplace/setManager?
validation_requirements: Review upgrade procedures and scripts for usage of initialize() on upgrades
Simulate initialization with zero/EOA settings and confirm marketplace becomes unusable
Confirm whether recovery setters are callable under the broken configuration

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During an upgrade, admin calls upgradeAndCall with initialize() parameters swapped/incorrect. MarketplaceSetting becomes an EOA; isFreeze staticcalls revert; all marketplace operations halt until another upgrade/fix. If setters depend on settings.s2Admin() and settings is wrong, recovery may be impossible without proxy admin intervention.
prerequisites: Upgrade operation or initial deployment misconfiguration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol downtime; potentially locked listings if operations cannot proceed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on proxy admin control and ability to re-upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing negative tests for initialization parameter validation and recoverability

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor marketplaceSetting/marketplace addresses and contract code size checks off-chain

threats.title: Marketplace doesFunctionExist("decimals()") check is unreliable and can be bypassed by tokens with fallback returning success, causing pricing denominator misuse
threats.hypothesis_description: doesFunctionExist performs staticcall to the selector and considers success=true as 'function exists'. A token contract with a permissive fallback can return success for any selector (including decimals()), yet decimals() may not be implemented or may return malformed data. Subsequent IERC20Extended(token).decimals() call can revert or return attacker-controlled garbage if the ABI decode succeeds, corrupting baseAmount computation and potentially enabling mispriced listings/purchases or DoS.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code uses doesFunctionExist as a gate before calling decimals(); this pattern is not a strong guarantee. Since token issuer controls the vesting token (StepVesting.token), a malicious issuer could deploy a token with odd fallback behavior to manipulate marketplace pricing math.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol allows vesting plans whose underlying ERC20 token has non-standard behavior (fallback returns success / malformed return data)
VestingDeployer permits deploying StepVesting against such a token address
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states 'ERC20' but also mentions supporting low/high decimals; no on-chain enforcement of standard decimals behavior; addCoin decimals validation is commented out.

proof_of_signal.pattern: doesFunctionExist uses staticcall(abi.encodeWithSelector(selector)) and only checks success; then decimals() is called and used in 10**decimals denominator

preconditions: Underlying vesting token contract can return success for decimals selector without proper implementation OR returns extreme decimals
A vesting plan is made sellable and listed on marketplace
ABI decode of decimals() returns a value (or causes revert/DoS)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Pricing math can be corrupted or forced to revert; listings/purchases can be DoSed or mispriced
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check on decimals; no return-data length checks; no try/catch around decimals() call

analysis_questions: What is IERC20Extended definition and how is decimals() decoded?
Can a token with fallback return 32 bytes to satisfy uint8 decode but be arbitrary?
Is there any upstream validation in VestingDeployer restricting token contracts?
validation_requirements: Create a mock token with fallback returning success for decimals() and observe listVesting behavior
Test decimals returning 255 to force 10**255 overflow/revert
Assess whether this can be weaponized by malicious token issuers to disrupt the marketplace

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious issuer deploys a vesting plan using a custom ERC20 whose fallback makes doesFunctionExist pass but returns extreme decimals. Sellers can list, but purchases revert (or baseAmount becomes 0), effectively freezing trading for that plan or creating misleading prices.
prerequisites: Ability to get such vesting plan sellable (token issuer + vesting deployer path)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS of trading for affected vesting plans; possible mispricing if decimals spoofed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires delisting plan or upgrading marketplace logic

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing fuzzing around decimals() behavior and interface detection

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor decimals values and revert rates on list/purchase per vesting plan

threats.title: Grantor releaseRate Miscalculation Enables Token Theft from Vesting Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: In transferVesting, the grantor's releaseRate is recalculated as totalAmount/numOfSteps instead of (totalAmount-amountClaimed)/(numOfSteps-stepsClaimed). This inflates the per-step claim rate, allowing a seller who has already claimed some steps to over-claim tokens belonging to other beneficiaries in intermediate steps before the final step reverts from underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: A marketplace seller can claim steps, list a small amount (triggering the buggy recalculation), then continue claiming at inflated rates. The overclaimed tokens are drawn from the vesting contract's balance—other beneficiaries' funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantorVesting.releaseRate = grantorVesting.totalAmount / numOfSteps — divides by total steps instead of remaining steps (numOfSteps - grantorVesting.stepsClaimed)

preconditions: Attacker has a vesting schedule with multiple steps
Attacker has claimed some steps (stepsClaimed > 0, amountClaimed > 0)
Other beneficiaries exist in the same vesting contract with unclaimed tokens
Vesting plan is sellable on marketplace

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After claiming 5 of 10 steps (500/1000 tokens) and transferring 200: inflatedRate=800/10=80, correctRate=(800-500)/5=60. Over 4 intermediate steps: claims 320 instead of 240, stealing 20 tokens from other beneficiaries.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Overclaim = (inflatedRate - correctRate) * (remainingSteps - 1). Scales with stepsClaimed ratio and number of remaining intermediate steps.

economic_questions: What is the maximum extractable amount given typical vesting parameters?
Can the attack be repeated across multiple transfer-claim cycles?
Does the marketplace listing flow (list → claim → eventually unlist/sell) create a clean extraction path?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: seller claims 8/10 steps, lists 50 tokens, claims remaining steps at inflated rate
Verify amountClaimed exceeds totalAmount after intermediate claims
Verify final step reverts due to underflow in totalAmount - amountClaimed
Measure net token drain from other beneficiaries

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Only needs existing vesting allocation (zero additional capital)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01 ETH gas for list + claim transactions
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 2-20% of transferred amount stolen per cycle depending on step ratio
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (requires waiting for vesting steps to elapse)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None - attack uses existing vesting allocation
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When most steps are already claimed but several remain
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Victims discover missing funds only when their safeTransfer reverts due to insufficient contract balance

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other beneficiaries in the same StepVesting contract
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of claimable tokens proportional to overclaim amount
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire vesting contract balance for other beneficiaries

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare inflated vs correct releaseRate across parameter space

threats.title: VestingManager stepsClaimed Contamination Corrupts Buyer Vesting Schedules
threats.hypothesis_description: VestingManager acts as intermediary holder during marketplace trades. Its _vestings record accumulates a single stepsClaimed from the FIRST seller who lists on a given vesting plan. All subsequent buyers inherit this potentially stale stepsClaimed value regardless of the actual seller's progress, causing buyers to receive incorrect vesting schedules.
threats.investigation_rationale: The first seller's stepsClaimed permanently defines the manager's stepsClaimed. If first seller listed at step 0, late-stage buyers (purchasing step-8 tokens) start from step 0, delaying their claims. If first seller listed at step 8, early-stage buyers get accelerated vesting. An attacker can observe the manager's state and strategically purchase underpriced accelerated vestings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _createVesting for existing beneficiary (VestingManager) only updates totalAmount and releaseRate, never updates stepsClaimed. First seller's stepsClaimed becomes permanent for all manager-intermediated transfers.

preconditions: Multiple sellers list tokens from the same vesting plan at different step progress levels
VestingManager already holds a vesting allocation (from a prior listing)
Buyer purchases from a seller whose stepsClaimed differs from the manager's

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _createVesting existing path: totalAmount += _totalAmount; releaseRate recalculated with MANAGER's stepsClaimed, not seller's
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If manager.stepsClaimed=8 (from first seller) and new seller listed at step 2, buyer inherits stepsClaimed=8 and can claim 80% immediately
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Buyer pays discounted price for step-2 tokens but receives step-8 vesting schedule, gaining immediate access to majority of tokens

economic_questions: Can an attacker manipulate the manager's stepsClaimed by being the first lister?
What is the economic impact of stepsClaimed mismatch for typical vesting parameters?
Does the claimable() final-step catch-all prevent total overclaim?
validation_requirements: Simulate: Seller A lists at step 8, Seller B lists at step 2, Buyer purchases Seller B's allocation
Verify buyer inherits stepsClaimed=8 and can immediately claim ~80% of purchased tokens
Calculate fair value vs realized value for the buyer

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Price of the listing being purchased
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Potentially receiving immediate claim on tokens that should vest over remaining period
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on stepsClaimed delta - could be substantial if delta is large
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction for purchase, then claim immediately

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient funds to purchase the listing
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When manager's stepsClaimed is high and a low-stepsClaimed seller lists at discount
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Vesting contract may become insolvent for other beneficiaries if combined with H001

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Sellers who listed tokens and buyers who receive wrong vesting schedules
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Buyer either gains or loses significant vesting time depending on direction
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All marketplace-traded vesting allocations on a given plan

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Manager's stepsClaimed vs seller's stepsClaimed delta
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace full listing→purchase→claim flow with different stepsClaimed values

threats.title: Referral Fee Calculated But Never Transferred to Referrer
threats.hypothesis_description: In _handleTransfers, referralFeeCost is computed and emitted in the Purchased event, but no actual token transfer to the referral address occurs. The entire buyerFeeTotal + sellerFeeTotal is sent to feeCollector, keeping the referral's share. The referral system is completely non-functional despite events suggesting otherwise.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code bug: the referral amount is calculated, a variable is set, it's emitted in events, but the transfer is missing. FeeCollector receives funds that should go to referrers. Any integrator building referral tracking will have incorrect accounting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: referralFeeCost computed at line 295-298, but feeCollectorTotal at line 300 = buyerFeeTotal + sellerFeeTotal (does not subtract referralFeeCost). No safeTransfer to _referral address exists.

preconditions: A purchase is made with a non-zero _referral address
Listing has no whitelist (listing.whitelist == address(0))
Buyer fee > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: referralFeeCost = buyerFeeTotal - (baseAmount * bfee * referralFee) / (BASE * BASE) — computed but never used in transfer
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: feeCollector silently receives referral portion. No transfer(referral, referralFeeCost) exists in code.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: FeeCollector gains 100% of referral rewards across all referred purchases. With referralFee=1000, ~90% of buyer fee should go to referrer but goes to feeCollector instead.

economic_questions: What total referral fees would be misdirected over expected marketplace volume?
Is the referral formula itself also inverted (90% vs 10%)?
Are there downstream integrators relying on Purchased event's referralReward field?
validation_requirements: Confirm no transfer to _referral exists anywhere in the purchase flow
Verify feeCollectorTotal does not deduct referralFeeCost
Test with actual purchase and verify referrer balance unchanged

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A — protocol design flaw, not attacker exploit
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: FeeCollector receives all referral fees — potentially significant protocol revenue leakage

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Referral program is non-functional, undermining user acquisition incentives

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All referrers who promote the marketplace expecting rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All referral fees across marketplace lifetime

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace exact token flows in _handleTransfers with referral set

threats.title: Self-Service Whitelist Bypass Undermines Private Sale Restrictions
threats.hypothesis_description: The whitelistAddress() function has no access control — any user can call it to add themselves to any whitelist. Private listing protection is reduced to a first-come-first-served capacity limit rather than seller-curated access control. Any attacker can whitelist themselves and purchase from private listings intended for specific buyers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Documentation states 'ensuring only authorized addresses can participate' but the code allows anyone to self-authorize. The lotOwner (seller) has no function to add specific addresses, only to increase capacity. This contradicts the stated design intent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: whitelistAddress() is external with no caller restriction — any address can whitelist itself up to maxWhitelist capacity

preconditions: A private listing exists with available whitelist slots
Attacker has funds to purchase the listing

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No access check: any msg.sender can set userSettings[msg.sender] = true
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls whitelistAddress() on the listing's whitelist contract, then purchases from the private listing
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Private listings often offer discounted prices to specific buyers. Attacker gains access to these favorable terms.

economic_questions: Are private listings typically offered at significant discounts vs public listings?
Can an attacker front-fill all whitelist slots, blocking intended buyers?
Is the self-service design intentional or a missing access control?
validation_requirements: Confirm non-lotOwner can call whitelistAddress()
Verify whitelisted address can purchase from private listing
Test that lotOwner has no mechanism to remove unauthorized whitelist entries

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Access to private-sale pricing, potentially significant discount
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on private vs public price differential

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before legitimate whitelist slots fill up

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Sellers who created private listings for specific counterparties
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of private sale exclusivity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All private listing value on the marketplace

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: End-to-end test: deploy whitelist, self-whitelist from attacker, purchase

threats.title: Reentrancy in spotPurchase via Callback-Enabled Currency Token
threats.hypothesis_description: In spotPurchase, _handleTransfers executes external safeTransferFrom/safeTransfer calls to the currency token BEFORE listing.balance is decremented. If the currency token supports transfer callbacks (ERC-777, hooks), an attacker can reenter spotPurchase during the callback and purchase the same listing multiple times, extracting vesting tokens beyond the listing balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated: external calls precede state updates. The addCoin function has no token validation (decimals check was commented out), so any token including callback-enabled ones could be added as supported currency.
deployment_assumptions: A callback-enabled token (e.g., ERC-777) is added as supported currency via addCoin()
threats.config_evidence: SecondSwap_Marketplace.sol:addCoin() lines 160-165 — decimals validation is commented out, no callback/hook detection. Any token can be added.

proof_of_signal.pattern: spotPurchase calls _handleTransfers (external token transfers at lines 287-305) BEFORE updating listing.balance at line 326. No reentrancy guard present.

preconditions: A callback-enabled token is added as supported currency
A listing exists using that currency
Attacker deploys a contract that reenters spotPurchase during transfer callback

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _handleTransfers external calls at lines 287-305, listing.balance update at line 326
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: During safeTransfer to seller (line 300), attacker's callback reenters spotPurchase. listing.balance unchanged from first call, so validation passes again.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each reentrant call extracts _amount of vesting tokens. Attacker drains VestingManager's allocation for the listing's vesting plan.

economic_questions: Does SafeERC20's safeTransfer trigger ERC-777 hooks?
Can the reentrant call's completePurchase succeed if VestingManager has sufficient allocation?
Would Solidity 0.8 underflow in listing.balance -= _amount on unwind catch the issue?
validation_requirements: Deploy ERC-777 token, add as supported currency
Create listing with ERC-777 currency, attempt reentrant purchase
Verify listing.balance unchanged during reentrant call

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Purchase price × number of reentrant calls
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.05 ETH gas for complex reentrant TX
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire listing's vesting allocation drained beyond intended balance
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on vesting token value vs purchase price

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after callback-enabled token is added
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: VestingManager drained of vesting allocation for affected plan

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Seller (listing oversold), other marketplace participants on same vesting plan
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of listed allocation beyond intended sale
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: VestingManager's entire allocation for the affected vesting plan

threats.title: Division by Zero Blocks End-of-Vesting Transfers to New Beneficiaries
threats.hypothesis_description: In _createVesting, when a new beneficiary receives a transfer and numOfSteps == _stepsClaimed (all steps elapsed), releaseRate = amount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) causes division by zero. This prevents transferring vesting tokens to new addresses after the full vesting period, blocking both marketplace purchases and direct transfers at end-of-vesting.
threats.investigation_rationale: Marketplace purchases at end-of-vesting (when all steps have elapsed but tokens remain unclaimed) will revert, locking tokens in VestingManager. The buyer pays but the completePurchase call fails.

proof_of_signal.pattern: releaseRate: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) — when _stepsClaimed == numOfSteps, divides by zero

preconditions: All vesting steps have elapsed (block.timestamp >= endTime)
Transfer target is a new address (no existing vesting record)
Grantor has unclaimed tokens

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) where both equal numOfSteps → division by zero
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any marketplace purchase after full vesting period reverts for new buyers. Listed tokens become permanently locked in VestingManager.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not profit-driven — funds locked permanently. Sellers cannot recover listed tokens if unlistVesting also triggers this path.

economic_questions: How many listings could be active near end-of-vesting?
Does unlistVesting also hit this path (transferring back to seller at end of vesting)?
Can existing beneficiaries (with existing vesting records) still receive transfers?

Verify division by zero revert
Test with existing beneficiary (should take else branch, no div-by-zero)

threats.title: Disabling Sellable Status Does Not Pause Active Listings
threats.hypothesis_description: The setSellable(false) function is intended to halt trading for a specific vesting plan. However, spotPurchase does not check the sellable status, meaning existing listings remain fully active and purchasable.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a token is compromised or an emergency halt is required, the admin will call setSellable(false). Because this only prevents new listings, attackers can still buy existing active listings, bypassing the intended emergency pause mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: spotPurchase calls _validatePurchase which only checks listing.status == Status.LIST, ignoring vestingSettings[plan].sellable

preconditions: Listings exist for a vesting plan
Admin calls setSellable(false) to halt trading

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker acquires tokens during an emergency event at stale prices

threats.title: Token Owner Assignment is Immutable and Restricts Multi-Token Issuers
threats.hypothesis_description: The setTokenOwner function maps an owner address to a token address and requires the mapping to be empty. This prevents an issuer from owning multiple tokens and prevents the admin from correcting mistakes.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an issuer launches a second token, they must use a different wallet. If an owner's keys are compromised, the admin cannot transfer ownership of the token to a new address, leaving the vesting management permanently compromised.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_tokenOwner[_owner] == address(0)); _tokenOwner[_owner] = token;

preconditions: Admin needs to assign a second token to an existing owner, or change the owner of an existing token

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Mapping is owner -> token, not token -> owner
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If keys are compromised, attacker retains permanent control over vesting reallocations

economic_questions: Can the admin bypass this by upgrading the deployer proxy?
How does this affect protocols with dual-token ecosystems?

threats.title: Unbounded Array in createVestings Leads to Out-of-Gas DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The createVestings function iterates over an unbounded array of beneficiaries, performing state updates and external token transfers for each. A large array will exceed the block gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Token issuers attempting to distribute vestings to hundreds of users in a single transaction will fail. Since the function pulls tokens via safeTransferFrom in a loop, it is highly gas-intensive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for loop over _beneficiaries.length calling _createVesting which does safeTransferFrom

preconditions: Token issuer calls createVestings with a large array (e.g., > 100 addresses)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(n) external calls in a single transaction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transaction reverts due to block gas limit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but operational failure for the protocol

Does the deployer contract batching exacerbate this?

threats.investigation_rationale: The marketplace relies on the settings contract to locate the vesting manager. If settings are upgraded without migrating the escrowed vestings, the new manager won't have the seller's tokens, causing purchases to revert and trapping listed assets.

threats.title: Implementation Contracts Missing _disableInitializers() Allow Direct Initialization
threats.hypothesis_description: SecondSwap_Marketplace, SecondSwap_VestingManager, and SecondSwap_VestingDeployer use OpenZeppelin's Initializable pattern but do not include constructors calling _disableInitializers(). The implementation contracts deployed behind TransparentUpgradeableProxy remain uninitialized. An attacker can call initialize() directly on the implementation contract, becoming admin. While Transparent proxy separates implementation state from proxy state, initialized implementation contracts are a known best-practice violation that could become exploitable if the upgrade pattern changes to UUPS, or if other contracts reference the implementation address directly.
threats.investigation_rationale: All three proxy-deployed contracts lack _disableInitializers(), confirmed by code inspection and deployment scripts showing separate implementation and proxy deployments.
threats.config_evidence: DeployVestingDeployerProxies.ts, DeployVestingManagerProxies.ts, DeployMarketplaceProxies.ts all deploy implementation separately from proxy

proof_of_signal.pattern: No constructor present; uses Initializable.initializer but never calls _disableInitializers() in a constructor

preconditions: Attacker can identify implementation contract address (deterministic via deployment tx or etherscan)
Implementation contract has not been previously initialized

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No constructor calling _disableInitializers() in any of the three Initializable contracts
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — attacker interacts directly with implementation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — implementation remains uninitialized until someone calls initialize()

operational_questions: Can the initialized implementation be leveraged for any attack under Transparent proxy pattern?
If upgrade pattern changes to UUPS, does this become critical?
validation_requirements: Call initialize() on implementation contract, verify success
Assess impact of attacker-controlled implementation state under current proxy pattern
Verify no cross-reference to implementation address in protocol

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify implementation contract addresses from deployment transactions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call initialize() on each implementation, setting attacker as admin
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Under current Transparent proxy, impact is limited (separate storage)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If protocol migrates to UUPS or if other contracts reference implementation, escalate attack

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Implementation contract admin captured; no direct proxy impact under Transparent pattern
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Limited under current architecture; high if upgrade pattern changes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Implementation cannot be re-initialized; must deploy new implementation

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — call initialize() on known address
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time before someone else initializes

threats.title: stepDuration=0 in StepVesting permanently locks all deposited tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: When a token issuer deploys a vesting plan via VestingDeployer.deployVesting() where numOfSteps > (endTime - startTime), the constructor computes stepDuration = (endTime - startTime) / numOfSteps = 0 via integer truncation. All subsequent calls to claimable() divide elapsedTime by stepDuration (0), causing a permanent revert. No beneficiary can ever claim tokens from this vesting plan.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect in the deployment path: deployVesting validates startTime < endTime and steps > 0, but never validates that stepDuration > 0. Any tokens deposited via createVesting into this plan are permanently locked with no recovery mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Constructor: stepDuration = (_endTime - _startTime) / _numOfSteps; claimable(): currentStep = elapsedTime / stepDuration — division by zero when stepDuration=0

preconditions: Token issuer calls deployVesting with numOfSteps > (endTime - startTime)
At least one beneficiary has tokens created in this vesting plan

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: stepDuration = (_endTime - _startTime) / _numOfSteps truncates to 0 when steps exceed duration in seconds
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Accidental or intentional misconfiguration at deployment time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All tokens deposited into the vesting contract are permanently locked — total loss for all beneficiaries

economic_questions: Is there any mechanism to recover tokens from a broken vesting plan?
Can the VestingManager or VestingDeployer extract tokens from a non-claimable plan?
Does the marketplace listing flow also revert (transferVesting calls within the same plan)?
validation_requirements: Deploy vesting with steps=200, duration=100s and confirm stepDuration=0
Attempt claim after startTime and confirm revert
Confirm no admin rescue path exists

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Direct profit for attacker is 0; total value at risk = all tokens deposited into this plan
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Griefing attack: unlimited damage relative to cost

threats.title: Admin toggle of setSellable resets tokenIssuer's maxSellPercent and custom fee overrides
threats.hypothesis_description: When a tokenIssuer sets maxSellPercent to 0 to prevent secondary market sales, the admin can override this by calling setSellable(vesting, false) then setSellable(vesting, true). The second call sees maxSellPercent==0 && sellable==true, triggering the default initialization block that resets maxSellPercent to 2000 (20%) and fees to -1 (global default). This unintended side effect allows the admin to circumvent tokenIssuer restrictions and reset any custom fee configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The setSellable function's default initialization condition (maxSellPercent==0 && sellable==true) conflates 'never initialized' with 'explicitly set to 0 by tokenIssuer'. This creates a privilege escalation where admin actions override tokenIssuer's explicit configuration. Per the trust model, tokenIssuers should control maxSellPercent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (vestingSetting.maxSellPercent == 0 && vestingSetting.sellable) { vestingSetting.maxSellPercent = 2000; vestingSetting.buyerFee = -1; vestingSetting.sellerFee = -1; } — condition triggers when tokenIssuer explicitly set maxSellPercent=0

preconditions: TokenIssuer has set maxSellPercent to 0 for a vesting plan
Admin calls setSellable(vesting, false) then setSellable(vesting, true)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: maxSellPercent resets from 0 (tokenIssuer's explicit config) to 2000 (default 20%)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin toggle of sellable status has unintended side effect on sell restrictions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Sellers can now sell up to 20% of original allocation despite tokenIssuer intending to block all sales

Verify buyerFee/sellerFee also reset to -1

threats.title: Marketplace freeze locks all listed vesting tokens with no emergency unlist path
threats.hypothesis_description: Both listVesting and unlistVesting in the Marketplace have the isFreeze modifier, which requires !isMarketplaceFreeze. When the admin freezes the marketplace, ALL listed tokens become inaccessible — sellers cannot unlist to recover their vesting allocations, and the admin cannot perform emergency unlisting since the modifier applies to all callers. VestingManager.unlistVesting is only callable by the marketplace (onlyMarketplace), so there is no alternative recovery path during freeze.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isFreeze modifier on unlistVesting creates a design flaw where emergency operations are blocked during the very state (freeze) when they are most needed. The VestingManager's unlistVesting function is exclusively gated by onlyMarketplace, leaving no admin bypass.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isFreeze modifier on unlistVesting prevents all callers (including admin) from unlisting during freeze; VestingManager.unlistVesting has onlyMarketplace with no admin bypass

preconditions: Admin freezes marketplace via setMarketplaceStatus(true)
At least one listing is active (status == LIST with balance > 0)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: isFreeze modifier: require(!isMarketplaceFreeze) blocks unlistVesting for all callers during freeze
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin freeze (possibly in response to emergency) traps all listed tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; all listed vesting allocations locked for freeze duration

threats.title: VestingManager and VestingDeployer setAdmin missing zero-address check allows permanent admin lockout
threats.hypothesis_description: VestingManager.setAdmin() and VestingDeployer.setAdmin() accept address(0) as the new admin, unlike MarketplaceSetting.setS2Admin() which explicitly checks for zero address. Setting s2Admin to address(0) permanently locks all admin functions on these contracts: setMarketplace, setVestingDeployer, setSellable (admin path), setBuyerFee, setSellerFee on VestingManager, and setTokenOwner, setManager on VestingDeployer. This effectively bricks critical protocol infrastructure with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inconsistency across the three admin contracts: MarketplaceSetting validates against zero address while VestingManager and VestingDeployer do not. This is a code defect — if one contract validates, the others should too. An accidental setAdmin(address(0)) call permanently disables all admin operations on the affected contract.
threats.config_evidence: MarketplaceSetting.setS2Admin has require(_user != address(0)) at line; VestingManager.setAdmin and VestingDeployer.setAdmin lack this check

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setAdmin(address _admin) external onlyAdmin { s2Admin = _admin; } — no require(_admin != address(0))

This could be accidental (typo, script error) or part of a grief attack by a compromised admin

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; permanently disables admin operations on affected contract, blocking marketplace configuration, vesting plan management, and fee updates

What operations become permanently blocked?

threats.title: VestingDeployer.ts deployment module appears misconfigured (constructor called with wrong args), risking broken vesting deployer setup
threats.hypothesis_description: The Hardhat Ignition module `modules/VestingDeployer.ts` deploys `SecondSwap_VestingDeployer` by passing only `[s2Admin]`, but the contract has no constructor and requires `initialize(address,address)` for setup. This suggests a real risk that production deployments via this module create a non-proxied, uninitialized contract or fail deployment, breaking ability to deploy vesting plans or manage token ownership mappings.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical ops risk: incorrect wiring/deployment yields a live system where critical paths (deployVesting, createVesting, transferVesting) are unusable or controlled by unintended parties. Needs verification of which scripts are actually used for production and whether proxies are used consistently.
deployment_assumptions: Team deploys using modules/VestingDeployer.ts (or derived scripts) rather than the Foundry script or the proxy module
The deployed instance is used as the authoritative vesting deployer in VestingManager/MarketplaceSetting
threats.config_evidence: modules/VestingDeployer.ts passes a single constructor arg to an Initializable contract; no constructor exists in SecondSwap_VestingDeployer.sol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: m.contract("SecondSwap_VestingDeployer",[ s2Admin ]); // mismatched with initialize(s2Admin, manager)

preconditions: Deployment uses the misconfigured Ignition module output as the live vesting deployer
Manager/marketplace wiring points to this deployed address
No subsequent successful initialize() was executed on the correct address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Inconsistent deployment approach: some modules use proxies with initData, but this one appears to deploy implementation directly with incorrect args.

threats.title: Token Issuer Can Arbitrarily Confiscate Any User's Vesting Allocations via transferVesting
threats.hypothesis_description: The tokenIssuer authorized on each StepVesting contract can call transferVesting with any _grantor address, unilaterally moving vesting allocations from any beneficiary to any other address without the grantor's consent. Similarly, the VestingDeployer's transferVesting function allows the token owner to specify arbitrary grantor addresses. While the project documentation states 'Token issuers must always be able to reallocate vestings' (confirming this is by design), marketplace buyers who purchase vesting allocations may not realize their purchased allocations can be confiscated by the token issuer at any time. There is no timelock, governance approval, or notification mechanism for such transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even though documented as intended, marketplace buyers have a reasonable expectation that purchased allocations are secure. The absence of any safeguard (timelock, multi-sig, event alerting) makes this a trust boundary concern for marketplace participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.sender == tokenIssuer || msg.sender == manager || msg.sender == vestingDeployer) — tokenIssuer can call with any _grantor, no consent required

threats.title: Misconfiguration/bug in deployment modules can wire Marketplace.initialize args incorrectly, leaving marketplace unusable or mis-administered
threats.hypothesis_description: Hardhat Ignition module DeployMarketplace.ts appears to pass constructor args to SecondSwap_Marketplace despite it having no constructor and requiring initialize(token, setting). Additionally, the argument order in DeployMarketplace.ts is (marketPlaceSetting.marketplaceSetting, token.token) which does not match initialize(address _token, address _marketplaceSetting). A mis-wired deployment can set marketplaceSetting to an ERC20 token address, breaking isFreeze/admin checks and potentially bricking all operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical production risk: incorrect wiring causes systemic DoS or wrong admin authority. Even though the Foundry deployment script is correct, multiple deployment paths exist and the risk should be validated operationally.

threats.title: setTokenOwner allows assigning multiple issuers to the same token, enabling conflicting vesting operations

threats.title: Token Issuer Can Arbitrarily Confiscate User Vesting Allocations
threats.hypothesis_description: The transferVesting function allows the tokenIssuer to specify any address as the grantor. A malicious or compromised token issuer can unilaterally transfer vested tokens from any user's address to their own, confiscating assets purchased on the marketplace.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a severe centralization risk and trust boundary violation. While token issuers are semi-trusted, giving them the power to rug-pull individual users undermines the security of the entire secondary market.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.sender == tokenIssuer || ...); ... _vestings[_grantor];

preconditions: Attacker is the tokenIssuer or has compromised the tokenIssuer's private key

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transferVesting does not require the grantor's approval or signature

operational_questions: Is this arbitrary transfer capability an intended feature for compliance/recovery, or a security flaw?
Are token issuers required to use multisig wallets to mitigate key compromise risks?
validation_requirements: Execute transferVesting as the tokenIssuer specifying a different user as the grantor
Verify the tokens are successfully moved without the grantor's consent

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Token issuer's private key is compromised
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls transferVesting on high-value user accounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker transfers the vesting allocations to their own address and claims the tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Massive loss of user funds, complete loss of protocol trust
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires hard fork or legal action

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden movement of large vesting allocations to a single address

threats.title: Marketplace Freeze Permanently Locks Listed Vesting Allocations
threats.hypothesis_description: The unlistVesting function uses the isFreeze modifier. If the admin freezes the marketplace during an emergency, users cannot unlist their tokens to recover them. Even the admin cannot unlist tokens on behalf of users because the modifier applies to all callers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Emergency pause mechanisms should typically allow users to withdraw their funds. By blocking unlisting, the protocol traps user assets during critical operational incidents.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function unlistVesting(address _vestingPlan, uint256 _listingId) external isFreeze

preconditions: Admin sets isMarketplaceFreeze to true
Users have active listings on the marketplace

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Withdrawal/unlisting function is blocked by the emergency pause modifier
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to freeze the marketplace

operational_questions: Was it intended to block unlisting during a freeze, or is this an oversight?
How can users recover their assets if the marketplace is permanently frozen?
validation_requirements: Freeze the marketplace as admin
Attempt to call unlistVesting as a seller and as the admin
Verify both attempts revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin freezes the marketplace due to a suspected vulnerability
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempt to withdraw their listed tokens to secure them
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transactions revert, leaving the tokens trapped in the potentially vulnerable VestingManager

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users cannot access their funds during emergencies
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - admin must unfreeze the marketplace to allow withdrawals

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed unlistVesting transactions during a freeze period
